prompts: Archimedes' principle states that any object submerged in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the object. This principle explains why objects float or sink. If the buoyant force is greater than the object's weight, it floats; if less, it sinks. The volume of displaced fluid directly corresponds to the volume of the submerged part of the object. Fluids, including liquids and gases, exert this buoyant force, demonstrating that buoyancy depends on fluid density and the volume of displaced fluid.
